data.title: SLVO: An Income Oriented Strategy For The Silver Trade
data.description:
The UBS ETRACS Silver Shares Covered Call ETN (SLVO) is an exchange-traded note designed to provide investors with monthly income derived from option premiums received through a covered call strategy on the iShares Silver Trust (SLV). With the price per ounce of silver reaching new highs, investors may need to weigh how the performance of SLVO is influenced by the price of silver and whether this ETN is appropriate for one’s portfolio strategy.

About UBS ETRACS Silver Shares Covered Call ETN

SLVO was issued by Credit Suisse on April 16, 2013 and is set to mature on April 21, 2033. The strategy was absorbed by UBS on May 31, 2024 when the two banks merged. On January 28, 2025, Nasdaq became the sole Index Sponsor of the Nasdaq Silver FLOWS 106 Index of which SLVO’s performance was designed to track. SLVO has an annual investor fee rate of 65bps.

Unlike exchange-traded funds, ETNs are debt issuances that represent the performance of the indicated strategy. In addition to this, ETNs mature, meaning that UBS will be obligated to return the notional value of the ETNs to the investors at the end of the term. The value received at maturity is based on the arithmetic average of the preceding 5 trading days upon the maturity date.

Rather than paying out a dividend distribution, the ETN pays out a cash coupon. The cash coupon is paid out monthly and may vary month-to-month based on the strategy’s call premiums received.

SLVO was designed to track the NASDAQ Silver FLOWS [Formula-Linked OverWrite Strategy] 106 Index. The Index was designed to measure the performance of a covered call strategy on SLV, which will perform based on the price returns of SLV shares along with option premiums received net of costs. Essentially, the Index will track a long position in SLV shares along with a short position in call options [short-long]. Call options are sold with a strike price of roughly 106% of the price of SLV shares, or 6% above the trading price when the option is sold. This means that if SLV appreciates beyond 6%, SLV may be called away, limiting the full upside potential of the long-SLV shares position.

What SLVO Is and How It Works

SLVO is essentially a buy-write strategy in which the strategy tracks the performance of the underlying shares while overlaying a covered call options strategy to generate income. As a result of the structure of the strategy, there will be some puts and takes when investing in SLVO.

Option premiums generate monthly income. Long SLV shares provide upside potential. Covered calls may limit upside potential if SLV shares appreciate beyond 6% before the options’ expiration date.

SLVO can be best applied in a flat-to-slow-growth silver market environment where investors may fully benefit from the value appreciation of the SLV shares while gaining income through the covered call strategy. As a result of the covered call strategy, SLVO may fall short in performance during periods of high volatility, particularly when silver appreciates beyond the 6% spread. Because of the bull run that began in September 2025, SLVO has significantly underperformed SLV. This can be observed when comparing 1-year total returns.

Comparing a 1-year period prior to September 2025, SLVO and SLV moved in near tandem, suggesting that the strategy may work best during more normalized trading patterns. Given the structure of the trading strategy, SLVO may outperform SLV during periods of flat, declining, and slow growth for silver prices.

Investor Suitability

SLVO may be most appropriate for buy-and-hold investors seeking monthly income. SLVO pays out monthly coupons; the total coupon rate for the last 12 months was $24.3777/note, which amounts to roughly 24% yield at the current SLVO.

SLVO may not be appropriate as an active investment strategy given the limited upside potential relative to SLV. SLVO has relatively thin liquidity with $344mm in net assets and an average of $4.9mm in value changing hands on a daily basis. This compares to SLV, which has roughly $57b in net assets and an average trading volume of $5.05b in share value.

Risks Related to SLVO

SLVO was designed to provide long exposure to SLV while overlaying a covered call options strategy to generate monthly income, presenting certain risks that investors should consider prior to making a final investment decision. SLVO limits upside potential as a result of the covered call strategy, particularly during periods of heightened volatility to the upside. We can see this in full force over this last year (at the time of writing), where SLV has enjoyed a sizable run-up while SLVO did not participate in the upswing, even when looking at total return (which incorporates reinvested income).

Given the ETN structure, SLVO will mature on April 21, 2033 with no guaranteed minimum payment at maturity. An investment in SLVO is not a direct investment in physical silver, but merely an exchange-traded product designed to track the performance of SLV and a covered call strategy on SLV shares. Given the variability feature of coupon payments, investors are not guaranteed to receive payments. Unlike bonds, investors will not receive the principal amount upon maturity; the ETNs may mature at a price below the investor’s initial investment.

Additionally, it's important to note that the income generated as a result of the short calls creates a volatility-muting effect as reflected in the total return. However, this is not a structural buffer against SLV's downside, as the strategy doesn't involve any long puts that would create a floor. The downside cushioning effect is limited to the income received.

Final Thoughts

SLVO is an ETN designed to provide investors exposure to silver through long exposure to SLV while selling covered call options on SLV to generate monthly income. SLVO may experience limited upside potential during periods of high price appreciation in SLV given the covered call strategy. SLVO may be best utilized during normal silver market operations as exhibited in the charts above.

This article answers three main questions about SLVO:

  1. How does SLVO relate to SLV and what is its purpose?
  2. What risk accompany SLVO?
  3. In which trading environments is SLVO most likely to perform well?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Hercules Capital: 7 Reasons Why This Is The Top BDC For 2026
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Hercules Capital (HTGC) is one of three business development companies assets that I recommend buying and that hold a place in my income-focused portfolio. Even though 2026 will most likely continue the cycle of rate cuts that started in 2025, HTGC is one of the few BDCs that can adapt to pressure from the Fed's monetary policy. In this article, I'll break down seven key factors that signal HTGC is a "Buy" in 2026. My conclusions are based not only on Hercules' ability to pay high dividends, and also on its potential for share price growth. I believe it's strategically important to select assets for an income-oriented portfolio that are capable of preserving capital, and therefore I focus primarily on total return rather than dividend yield [FWD].

Reason #1: Focus on venture lending

Hercules' business model is based on providing venture loans to innovation-oriented companies, which are mostly small and medium-sized businesses. Lower interest rates create potential for the venture market to grow, meaning more creditworthy projects will pop up that can get loans. The potential expansion of Hercules' asset portfolio means that, even with the short-term risks of lower interest rate spreads, the long-term outlook is positive for BDC business growth. Moreover, in 2025, venture lending reached a new high of $62.4 billion, signaling positive momentum in the industry. HTGC's lending strategy is based on providing access to financial capital for companies engaged in revolutionary developments but without a high level of financial stability and income history. While venture lending is an alternative financing product, its potential to replace bank lending remains limited. One reason for this is the careful selection process involved in identifying innovative companies eligible for venture debt.

Reason #2: Effective internal management

Of all the BDCs, HTGC is one of the few that uses an internal management model. This is a definite plus, in my opinion, since it helps save money when scaling up the portfolio and operations. HTGC does not have to pay additional fees for the work of external advisors. Typically, such expenses in the BDC industry amount to 2% of assets and 20% of profits. As a result, HTGC, as well as other internally managed companies, are able to increase net profits for further distribution in the form of payments to shareholders. This is why shares of BDC companies with an internal management structure tend to trade at a higher premium than companies with external management. More detailed comparisons of internal and external management are presented in the table.

Characteristics

Internal management

External management

Management fee

None

Typically, 2.0% of assets

Success fee

Replaced with bonuses

Usually, 20% of profit

Scalability

High (costs per asset are falling)

Low (commission is fixed at a percentage)

Premium to NAV

Often trades at a high premium

Often trades closer to NAV

Reason #3: Profit expansion through warrants

HTGC venture lending allows for more active participation in the strategic development of borrower companies and the commercialization of their innovations into high-demand products. In this context, Hercules receives payments for the use of borrowed funds not only in cash form, but also in the form of warrants when providing venture lending. The latter instrument allows HTGC to earn money when a borrower company goes public. Depending on the growth in market value, additional profits can be generated. Based on data for Q3 2025, the HTGC has 102 warrants with a GAAP fair value of $40.7 million. They had a face value of $63 million. Assume that 50% of the warrants won't be cashed in, then their value is $17.1 million.

Reason #4: High credit rating

When I looked at HTGC and its competitor with a similar business model, Trinity Capital (TRIN), I paid special attention to how these two companies manage to keep their returns so high. Even though TRIN had a higher dividend yield (more than 3%), it didn't mean that its financial policy was more attractive to shareholders. In fact, it's Trinity that provides such payments thanks to a higher debt load, whilst HTGC's key metric, debt-to-equity, is only 0.98x (TRIN's is 1.18x). From one perspective, the Hercules leverage policy negatively impacts dividend yields, which become lower. From another perspective, the lower yield enables HTGC to maintain a high credit rating, enabling it to attract external financing at a lower cost. As an example, the Moody’s agency raised the company’s credit rating to Baa2 in Q3 2025.

Reason #5: Hercules is the "blue chip" of BDC

The high valuation of HTGC among market participants has led to this asset being called the "blue chip" of the BDC sector. The company is the best in the venture lending industry, and its shares are trading at a premium to P/NAV of around 1.50x. That is, it costs about $1.50 of capital to buy $1 of Hercules' portfolio assets. This investment is not as profitable as trading shares at their NAV par value. But this valuation shows that the market really values HTGC's management and portfolio quality. This is why this asset is considered one of the best in BDC.

Reason #6: High dividend stability

Current HTGC dividend yield is 10.13%, with an average annual growth rate of 4.56% over the past five years. Meanwhile, the average dividend yield over a four-year period was 11.47%, having declined slightly due to rising share prices. Stability of HTGC dividends is ensured by the high profitability of the company's portfolio, as was the case even in 2020. At least 90% of its taxable income must be distributed as dividends. Consequently, if earnings exceed the base dividend, then a special dividend is paid, with the amount for the last 4 quarters being $0.07 per share.

Reason #7: Chart analysis points to continued growth in HTGC

In the first place, HTGC is an asset for income-focused investment portfolios. As I mentioned in the introduction to this article, however, when selecting assets, in addition to high dividend yields, the ability to preserve capital continues to be a key factor for me. As such, total return is a key indicator for me. In this regard, the technical analysis of the higher time frames of HTGC clearly demonstrates room for more growth in the company's shares. As an example, we can see price movement within an upward trend channel on the 1D chart. Growth is expected to continue, though this will require a breakout of the resistance zone formed by liquid pools ($19.13–$19.67).

When we look at the 1W chart, HTGC shares continue to move within a global upward trend channel. Furthermore, they're closer to the lower boundary than the upper one, pointing to significant upside potential. On top of that, they have a support zone ($15.65–$16.95) that continues to keep them from falling. The last time the price dropped to this level was in early October. In fact, Oct. 9, 2025, marked the first time I published an article about HTGC, which was followed by a 9.10% total return on this purchase, with 6.25% of that coming from share growth.

HTGC risks

One of the main risks when investing in HTGC shares is the high premium to NAV as this means that any systemic market reversals could bring the upward trend to an end. As market participants seek to revalue their portfolios, those assets with the highest premiums are often sold, because they have a fairly wide range of potential downside. Such a scenario could occur, however, if, rather than the projected 50 basis point cut in the Fed's interest rate in 2026, a more aggressive 100 basis point cut were to occur. It could be the turning point that stops the current rise in stock prices and causes HTGC's value to drop. We also need to pay close attention to the company's next quarterly reports, which will be important for monitoring key metrics such as NAV, NII, and non-accruals. Even though Hercules manages its portfolio effectively, this cycle of rate cuts is still a systemic trigger that could lead to a revaluation of BDC companies.

Conclusion

After conducting a fundamental analysis of HTGC's investment attractiveness, I identified and examined seven key reasons why my recommendation for this asset is "Buy." Even though the BDC sector has historically been affected by falling interest rates, I think a company like Hercules Capital can not only handle changing market conditions well but also stay popular with investors. A dividend yield of 10% is likely to remain in place in 2026. A technical analysis of senior charts suggests that the upward trend is likely to continue. Therefore, as many BDC assets lose value, the HTGC is one of the "blue chips" that can be added to an income-oriented investment portfolio.

data.title: ABB: Like Much Of The Market, Overvalued For 2026E
data.description:
As an honest value-oriented investor, my room for arguing that my ratings of "HOLD" because of valuation are valid, is limited by the subsequent performance that the company in question manages. ABB (ABBNY) is a complex thesis. In my last article, over a year ago, I gave a "HOLD" rating. You can find that article here. I will argue that the valuation was the right one, based on the early 2025A drop in valuation, when the company moved from over 50 CHF to less than 40.20 CHF, a valuation drop of 20%. At that time, the company was, for the briefest of times, at a fair valuation that could have justified a "BUY". I did not update at that time however, my focus was on other businesses. I also didn't buy ABB ltd, even though I looked into doing this.

Since that time, the company is up very significantly and is now an, in my opinion, an undisputed part of the general inflation in valuation that's characterizing the broader market. ABB, which we'll look at more closely in the valuation segment of my article, is trading at a P/E that's above anything it's ever traded at. Furthermore, at any time that the company has traded at such a multiple, the following declines have all but made sure that anyone who invested at the time locked in multi-year underperformance for his or her portfolio. Take the example from GFC-crash to about before Covid-19.

This means that for almost 12 years, invested capital did not even generate positive returns simply because of at the price you invested in. This is the heart of valuation-oriented/value-oriented investing - and it's one I follow.

So if you bought ABB in a way that allows you to take advantage of the current overvaluation, my congratulations to you - but I will now spend this article trying to convince you to look at the details of your position, the market, and the company, to see if you may or should consider a rotation of your position.

Let's see what we have here. One change is that I will look at the Swiss ticker instead of the Swedish one as a native because we can now, in Europe, invest freely in Switzerland again

ABB - Update for 2026E does not imply good returns in anything but a continued overvaluation

Asea Brown Boveri, commonly known as ABB, is a major global tech and industrial business. What it does is electrification and automation, which puts it into brackets with players like Siemens (SIEGY), Legrand (LGRDY) and others - all of which I cover, though it's often not a 100% equivalence. For instance, ABB's segment handling industrial robotics as well as the motion segment (motors and generators) differs it somewhat from many of these - however, here it's also important to note that ABB has decided to divest its robotics division entirely.

ABB is a beyond-solid fundamental business with over 135 years of operating history and a presence in over 100 countries. It's A-rated, yields 1.47% at the time of writing, and I believe it fair to say that only the most serious macro-and-general market turndowns could seriously affect the company and business.

Is such an event impossible to occur? No, it's happened many times in history. Can we forecast it wit any degree of accuracy?

Not in the least, unfortunately. All I can state with conviction is that the company is currently, by every comparable metric, significantly overvalued.

The theme and topic of today's article is 4Q25, because it was reported today. 2025A was a very solid year, and 4Q25 was the cherry on top. Orders were up significantly, even on an organic basis, revenue was up double digits (reported), and EBITA was up nearly 20%. 2025 was a record year, driven by demand in above all company solutions for electrification, with data centers and AI-oriented projects being very major contributors. The company has, as of the quarter, announced both a dividend increase as well as more share buybacks (the latter being a peak-poor-decision in my view).

I believe the investors and analysts who call for ABB to continue to trade higher, or even trade at this level, are betting for this level of orders to continue or to even grow further, while the company is expected to be traded according to similar multiples as today.

I view such expectations as very problematic. Despite 4Q25 highlights including massive order and backlog growth, EBITDA margin growth and new partnerships with updated targets, I view this valuation as completely disregarding the potential for a market downturn. Despite the company's improved plans, including stricter focus on performance and the "ABB Way", there's a limit to what can be achieved - even with the focus on AI and digital that we're seeing across the board and across the market.

The reason(s) that I believe an underperformance is imminent are several. I cite the obvious overvaluation, both P/B, PEG and P/E, I argue that segments like ABB India are showing weaknesses with a double-digit 21% drop in net profit related to increases in costs, and I argue that the current forecast doesn't take into consideration things like weakness in key markets, such as China.

I also believe the sale of the robotics segment to be a mistake on the part of the company. This is not the first time that ABB has made such a move that I view as dilutive and negative - they did the same thing with its Power Grid unit to Hitachi back in -18, and this has obviously worked out very well for the buyer. The sale of robotics may in fact result in ABB missing out on an AI growth segment, namely robotic AI. It's an exit at the "start", as even AI bulls will argue. To give an idea just how bad the sale to Hitachi was - the segment that Hitachi bought saw a 70%+ revenue growth in very short time, all related to renewables and electrification. ABB completely missed out on this growth - and I believe it's repeating its mistakes. Do you think that the cited $5.4B sales price reflects the true value of a 50+ year old robotics segment?

I don't. That's even without mentioning that ABB might put itself in a place of dependence going forward. I don't believe the future forecasts take into consideration this possibility.

Bottom line is that I don't believe the estimate of growth "gels" with the estimate of returns and margins and the company valuation, and where it is today. ABB might be justified at a higher than 17-18x P/E, but a 30x P/E is going to the realm of overvaluation - clear overvaluation.

Valuation for ABB - not justifiable here

To be clear, I view the company as a great business with great fundamentals, an absolute "BUY" at the right price. But I argue that several valuation-related factors, as mentioned above, are currently not being included in forecasts and prospects.

I would further add to this that the company, despite its extremely lofty valuation, has just committed to a significant, $2B+ buyback program. The result of this will be muddling the valuation further, as the company's own purchases will increase the market value of the business (or prevent a drop), without really anything changing except the number of shares outstanding.

Investing here means that you're committing to the high expectations set for the share price and for growth. If and when ABB fails to deliver those growth rates or expectations, you are in turn likely to see the company go down. Quickly.

How quickly?

Look at what happened in mid-2025. The company's trajectory from over 50 CHF to less than 41 CHF took less than 14 days- it took then more than 3 months for recovery, and we've been climbing since. And that was a drop that wasn't even related to operational challenges or less growth, just the fear that growth might be less due to tariffs.

I say that the likelihood of ABB underperforming the lofty expectations currently injected into the share price/valuation is high. Historical averages in valuation call for multiples of just below 20x. The return, when forecasting here, is negative - even with the dividend included in the return. And that is with an almost 10% annualized AEPS growth rate.

Consequently, if you want to have a market-beating upside of 15% annualized or above, ABB would have to have a forward valuation (again, based on an annualized AEPS growth of about 10% until 2028) of at least 33-35x P/E. The company is not trading here today, has never traded there, and is, in my view, very unlikely to ever trade there for any period of time. Even forecasting at current P/E, the returns annualized would be only 10.6% per year. I view this as being insufficient, and because of this, I give the company a "HOLD" rating here.

In terms of valuation, I would put the company at a conservative 20-22x P/E, close to the company's 20 year average. I acknowledge growth of 5-9% per year in AEPS, which implies a fair value for 2028E of about 58 CHF, about 3 CHF below the current share price. Of course, this is the fair value - for the price target, we're looking for a 15% annualized rate of return to this fair value. To get there, we'd need to buy at 48 CHF or below, and also include a 2-3% dividend growth rate per year.

So my PT for the company is 48 CHF for the ABBN Swiss ticker, which comes to an ADR target for ABBNY of $62/share. Risks are as follows.

Risks & Considerations

There are both execution-related and valuation-related risks to ABB here. On the operational side, I believe the sale of robotics is an echo of the sale of the -18 segment to Hitachi, which caused ABB to miss out on over 70% annualized revenue growth in renewables. I believe robotics will be one of the future's likely strongest AI-segments, and I believe therefore that Softbank has made a very good deal, leaving ABB with a lot less value. The $5.4B was not enough of a price for this segment - not even close, if AI bulls are to be believed (and even I can see some upside here).

However, the primary risk to the company is not AI or segments, but valuation. The company's valuation has reached herculean proportions, and now trades at almost 30x P/E, warranting an over 33x P/E to give a 15% annualized upside. The risk when the company doesn't achieve this is a drop - as we've seen before, and I consider upside here to be unlikely.

Thesis

While this company is without a doubt one of the largest industrials in the entire world, with an undeniable, attractive portfolio under its belt (even following the robotics sale), it's still - as of 2026 compared to earlier - not only hampered by its valuation, but hamstrung. I believe by investing in this company at this price, you expose yourself to significant downward risk.

The best company in the world won't matter if all you make is less than, or equal to inflation for the next years, and in this case, I believe the most likely outcome for ABB investors are negative returns, not positive ones. Based on current estimates and criteria, I would say that ABB warrants a PT of around 48 CHF at most, and beyond this, it is not worth investing in. Because it trades at over 60 CHF at the time of writing, I am saying this is not an appealing investment. Even with a fair value of about 58 CHF, this is the price where we'd want to look for an exit, not an entry.

I believe you can do better than ABB today. I want to, and I want you to make better money and that's why I say that this company is a "HOLD". This is especially true in the current market situation.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a valuation target of 48 CHF/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of January 2026.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: GE Vernova: The Impressive Quarter Deserves A Rating Upgrade
data.description:
Introduction

It has been a couple of high-stakes months for GE Vernova (NYSE: GEV) since I last reviewed it back in late October. The company has transitioned from a massive grid-leader acquisition to now navigating significant political and operational hurdles in its wind division. I will go through the company’s recent earnings to assess if the company is still egregiously overvalued or if the narrative for its power and electrification segments has changed

Current Dynamics

GEV just released its quarterly earnings, and there is a lot to take away. It reported a shocking $13.39 in, which I first believed was a typo as estimates stood at around $3.2. Though it is important to dive deeper and understand that the beat was largely driven by a massive $2.9B one-time tax benefit. On the revenue front, the company saw sales reach $11B for the quarter, up around 4% Y/Y, slightly beating expectations and pushing the FY 2025 revenue figure to hit $38.1B. Even without the tax hit, the performance was relatively robust with orders surging by 65% in the quarter to $22.2B, pushing the total backlog to a staggering $150B. Looking at FCF, it has more than doubled in 2025 to $3.7B, proving that the company is able to generate significant cash even while fixing its Wind business.

Diving into GEV's segments, the big winner has been Electrification, which saw its revenue jump by 32% in Q4 thanks to apparently insatiable demand for grid equipment from data hyperscalers. This has pushed EBITDA margins to hit 17.1%, up from 13%, which has made it the most profitable part of the business right now. On the Power side, orders were up 77% thanks to the slot strategy. The company has 83GW of turbine capacity already reserved by utilities through 2026/2027. Nuclear also saw a boost as small modular reactors and traditional nuclear services gained traction in the clean firm power conversation. On the wind side, which has generally been the anchor of the business, the segment lost $225MM in the quarter with CFO Ken Parks identifying an up to $400MM revenue risk for 2026, specifically tied to the federal work stoppages. If the ships are not moving by March, the revenue for the year will evaporate as those specialized installation vessels are booked years in advance by other companies. This is a real head scratcher and should be closely monitored by investors. Investors have also turned fairly bullish based on management raising its 2026 targets despite the current wind drama, and also on the accelerated closing of the Prolec GE deal. Total revenue is set to exceed $44B to $45B, compared to the previous $41B and $42B predicted earlier. On the FCF side, it is expected to reach between $5B and $5.5B while the EBITDA margin is set to stay in the same range of 11 to 13%.

As much as I have my doubts on the company’s valuation, it is fair to say that it was a relatively good quarter for GEV, even with the wind headache. Though the market initially pushed GEV way higher on the release, the digestion period has slimmed down the gains as investors had time to read between the lines of the superb quarterly EPS figure. The core Grid + Gas story is what is driving the $150B backlog and should continue to push the company’s stock higher. This has been the main story for GEV as it announced earlier a partnership with Chevron/Engine No.1 as it intends to build 4 GW of power foundries specifically for data centers. These will be off-grid natural gas plants that will power AI facilities directly, bypassing the aging public grid.

Valuation

My main issue with GE Vernova has always been the gigantic valuation given to the company without it benefiting from the high margins of the tech industry. Last time I reviewed the firm, its P/E stood at 141.2 while now it stands at 40.23, though I believe that it is skewed by the one-time tax benefits, as the company’s EV/EBITDA increased to 79.43. GEV has generated the highest levels of FCF in its recent history, but this only translates to a mere FCF yield of 2% due to the relentless rise in the stock price. Even based on the lofty guidance, the company is having an FWD PE Non-GAAP of 54.22, which is vastly expensive in my book for an industrial company. Though the company is certainly appealing to investors with its $10B buyback program and the doubling of its dividend, signalling that it has more cash than it knows what to do with.

GEV is currently benefiting mightily from the electrification supercycle, mainly from the AI boom. Hyperscalers are currently bypassing traditional utility timelines and ordering grid equipment directly from GEV to power their AI data centers. The Prolec GE acquisition also makes GEV capture the entire value chain for the electrical grid and it will be important to follow up and see how it will impact margins ahead. There is also something to be said about the race to commercialize small modular nuclear reactors with the BWRX-300 that is the only SMR technology currently under construction in the Western world. It cleared critical regulatory hurdles in the UK and secured a $400MM DOE grant to accelerate the commercial site in Tennessee. It could become a real growth story for the company as we approach the 2030s. The main drag still remains Wind, especially offshore, as US political and regulatory delays and manufacturing quality fixes will continue to be a loss-making endeavor for the company.

The story continues margin-wise with a sort of two-speed performance, with Power and electricity drastically improving their margins. In the gas power business, customers are now paying 10 to 20% higher prices just to reserve a manufacturing slot for 2027 and 2028, hence why the company gave a long-term forecast towards 2028. This all means that the revenue backlog coming in today is significantly more profitable than the current sales of turbines. The services backlog has now grown to $70B, which will carry a higher margin than the original equipment. Once the wind business stops bleeding, the EBITDA margin guidance is set to reach 20% in 2028. Now, as I mentioned, if the work orders are not fully resolved in late March, this would further wipe up to $400MM off the 2026 books. More so, as many are touting tech like valuations, the company has the lowest EBITDA margins of its peers.

Conclusion

I am dubious about the company’s lofty valuation, but I can’t overlook the current momentum that GE Vernova has seen and how it is positioning itself at the center of a huge power demand related to AI consumption. I want to see how it sells off its backlog in the next couple of years, but I have to be fair and upgrade my rating to a Hold for GE Vernova.

data.title: The Commodity Boom May Send Inflation And Rates Much Higher
data.description:
Market dynamics seem to change really quickly these days. It was only a few weeks ago that it seemed inflation might be beaten, but now the market has changed its mind, as it has a right to, as the information has changed.

CPI Inflation swaps are again pricing in higher inflation rates in the futures, with 1, 2-, and 5-year rates surging over the past several trading sessions. While they are still well below their previous highs, the surge is notable and suggests that the market is taking the sudden rise in commodity prices seriously and its potential impacts.

Oil may hold the key to how high and quickly inflation expectations rise, and whether the impact translates into higher interest rates.

Inflation Expectations Are Rising

There is good reason to think that if the prices of metals such as copper, silver, and gold surge as they have, inflation is likely to be pushed into manufacturing and services prices again. Certainly, the relationship between inflation, as reflected in the ISM manufacturing Services prices paid index, and copper has been very strong. It seems to be pretty visible to the naked eye, without even having to do much work.

The same is true when examining copper prices (HG1:COM) and comparing them with the producer price index. Again, there appears to be some timing and lag between the two, but they generally trend in the same direction for a period of time and peak and bottom around the same time.

One could imagine that if oil prices rise significantly and join the commodity boom, inflation expectations and fears could increase substantially, likely pushing them higher. Again, the relationship between oil (CL1:COM) and CPI swaps is fairly strong, and they have a long history together.

Impact on Rates

If the market is truly starting to get worried about inflation, then interest rates are bound to go higher as well. Over the last 4 years, interest rates have traded lockstep with oil prices, which is probably the main driver of why each time interest rates approach a breakout, they fail, primarily due to the steady decline in oil prices.

Oil prices since September 2022 have tended to peak and bottom ahead of 2-Yr Treasury rates, perhaps because of the volatility nature of oil, Treasuries wait to see if the move in oil is real, or perhaps Treasuries are just not good at following the price action, whatever the case, the recent surge in oil, would suggest that 2-year Treasury rates (US2H) probably head higher, if the recent break out in oil is real and sustainable.

The risk of inflation reentering the equation is attracting market attention, and it is first manifesting in inflation swaps. If commodity price movements persist or remain elevated, those prices are likely to feed into economic data, with oil being the primary driver, as it translates into CPI and PPI quickly.

Oil appears to be the key at this point, and if the move higher continues or builds momentum, then one knows what to watch for.

data.title: ESAB Corporation Doesn't Justify An Upgrade At This Time
data.description:
One company that I have found to be interesting for quite some time now is none other than ESAB Corporation (ESAB). The reason why I find this enterprise to be interesting is because its business model is relatively boring. In essence, it develops, produces, and supplies consumable products and equipment that can be used in the cutting, joining, and automated welding activities that make the modern world run. Long ago, I was actually bullish about the business. But after seeing shares essentially double the S&P 500 in the time that I was bullish, I downgraded it. Since my most recent article about the company, an article in which I reaffirmed it as a "Hold" candidate, the stock has unfortunately woefully underperformed the market. Shares are down 0.6% compared to the 23.3% increase that the S&P 500 saw over the same window of time. However, since I originally downgraded it to a "Hold" in August 2024, the stock has risen 18.5%. That's not too far off the 22.4% increase that the market enjoyed.

Fundamentally speaking, the business is doing well on the top line. But bottom-line results have come under pressure. This helps explain the recent underperformance that the company has exhibited. But even so, management expects revenue to expand this year. Unfortunately, because profitability is dropping, the stock is becoming more expensive. Relative to other similar firms, shares are actually still very affordable. But that's not enough to justify an upgrade at this time.

A Mixed Bag

One of the best things about ESAB Corporation is that the company continues to grow on the top line. Take the most recent quarter as an example. This would be the third quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. During that time, revenue amounted to $727.8 million. That's 8.1% above the $673.3 million management reported a year earlier. This rise in sales was driven primarily by acquisition activities. You see, management has a history of acquiring other enterprises to grow.

The most notable example as of late involves the company's purchase of EWM GmbH, which operates as a big player in the heavy industrial welding equipment and advanced automation space. It specifically focuses on the EMEA (Europe, Middle East, and Africa) and Asia Pacific regions. This was acquired in August of last year at a cost to shareholders of $334.7 million. However, management has acquired multiple other enterprises during this time, with a combined purchase price of $117.7 million.

These acquisitions added $33.8 million to the top line for the company. That's a 5% boost to revenue. But there were other sources as well. Foreign currency fluctuations added $9.8 million, or 1.5%, to the top line. And existing businesses achieved organic growth amounting to $11 million. That's 1.6% of top-line growth. Management attributed this to higher prices and increased volume. But beyond that, they didn't really go much in the way of detail.

It is worth noting that the company does also provide data that strips out its operations in Russia. These sales amounted to $40.4 million in the most recent quarter. But even without this being factored in, revenue still increased 8.1% year over year, climbing from $635.6 million to $687.4 million. And once again, acquisitions did the heavy lifting here, adding $33.8 million in revenue to the business.

As great as it was to see revenue improve, profitability took a bit of a hit. Net income fell from $68.2 million to $54.8 million. Some of this was because of a contraction in the company's gross profit margin from 37.7% to 37%, with management blaming that on tariff-related cost increases that ultimately pushed margins lower. It is important to note that around 80% of the company's manufacturing is located in the region in which the sales associated with those manufactured goods are ultimately achieved. This is great, but it doesn't mean that the company is immune from tariffs. The firm specifically mentioned multiple times in its latest earnings call that it continues to look for ways to cut costs, including potential changes to its supply chain, higher prices aimed at offsetting margin compression, and various productivity improvements. The firm is also integrating AI into its workflow to cut expenses.

Other profitability metrics for the business also need to be considered. Take operating cash flow as an example. It dropped from $101 million to $81.5 million. If we strip out changes in working capital, we would get a drop from $91.6 million to $80.7 million. Finally, EBITDA for the business did improve, rising from $124.8 million to $133.4 million. The chart above shows financial results for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time in 2024. And as the two charts below illustrate, revenue growth was driven primarily by acquisition activities. But once again, because of issues here at home, profitability has taken a hit. This does not necessarily bode well for the near term and likely helps to explain why share price performance has been abysmal as of late.

The good news is that management believes that revenue growth for 2025 in its entirety will come in at between 4.5% and 5.5%. They also provided guidance for EBITDA of between $535 million and $540 million. At the midpoint, that would be above the $528.8 million reported for 2024. So even though some financial figures have worsened, at least that is on the rise. If we project out results based on what we have seen already for the year, net profits would be around $237.2 million while EBITDA would amount to $317.6 million.

To put these figures into perspective, I created the chart below. In it, you can see that the stock is actually very expensive. I wouldn't say that it's so expensive that it justifies a bearish outlook. But when I look at a company that's trading this high, I do wonder how much downside exists if conditions deteriorate. There is some positive here, which is the fact that the stock is attractively priced compared to other similar firms. The aforementioned chart illustrates this. On a price-to-earnings basis, as well as on an EV-to-EBITDA basis, only one of the five companies ended up being cheaper than it is. Meanwhile, on a price-to-operating cash flow basis, our prospect was the cheapest of the group.

Takeaway

As much as I would love to be bullish about ESAB Corporation right now, I must say that I fear how pricey the stock is. Yes, shares might be cheaper than the shares of other similar firms. But as somebody who prioritizes absolute value over relative value, this does concern me. I do appreciate the revenues growing, not only because of acquisitions but also because of organic revenue expansion. That was especially true in the most recent quarter. But this is not enough to offset the valuation concerns. As such, I believe that reaffirming the business as a "Hold" candidate is the right move.

data.title: Capcom: Earnings Are Growing Even Before The Big Releases
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I assign Capcom Co., Ltd. (CCOEY) (CCOEF) a strong buy rating due to another excellent quarter of earnings and upcoming catalysts that position the company to further accelerate its earnings going into Q4. Capcom is a company whose earnings growth is not dependent upon blockbuster releases, since older titles dominate game sales and drive its operating margins above 60% per the company's digital contents segment of revenue. They also have a very diversified IP model that consists of video games, amusement equipment, arcade machines, licensing, and media—all of which work in tandem to extend the lifecycle of its franchises and reduce the company's earnings volatility, especially compared to their pure-play publishing peers. And, with roughly 38% upside, as calculated from my updated price target for the company, Capcom continues to be undervalued by the market, especially given the fact that they are a low capital-intensive business, with excellent capital efficiency and are able to convert a lot of their operating earnings into cash for further reinvestment.

Earnings Recap

I've genuinely been looking forward to Capcom's earnings, and I was not surprised to see another strong quarter for the company. But given the company's latest history of broad-based growth across all company segments, should investors really have expected anything else? Let's get into the company's major metrics.

Major Metrics

Starting with net sales, the company is up 30% YoY to ¥115.3 billion, while operating profit is up 75% YoY to ¥54.3 billion, which lifted the company's operating margin to a nice 47.1%. Note that this is way above both last year's level of 34.9% and exceeds the company's full-year margin guidance of 38.4%. Ordinary profit also increased 65% YoY to ¥51.7 billion, while the net profit belonging to Capcom's shareholders is up 69% YoY to ¥38.8 billion.

The Digital Contents Segment of Revenue

Digital contents deserves the most focus because it still remains the company's main engine of revenue, generating 63.7% of the company's total revenue for the first nine months. For this segment, sales are up 25% YoY to ¥73.4 billion, while operating profit is up 58% YoY to ¥46 billion. This, in turn, lifted the company's operating margins to 62.8%.

The main driver for this segment of revenue, as it was last quarter, was catalog performance, totaling 33.39 million units in sales and growing 16.7% YoY, with older games representing 96.4% of total game units sold from catalog titles. Adding to this was digital unit sales—the most important metric given the profit margins for digital contents—reaching a rounded 33.61 million units and up 17.1% YoY and which accounts for 94.1% of total units sold.

It's worth noting that new title sales declined YoY, but this was expected given that major releases are scheduled for Q4 (which I discuss further below). And we can't forget that the company's increasing sales of older catalog titles, as previously mentioned, means higher margins for the company since development costs were already incurred in previous years, with lower marketing expenditures needed for older titles, thereby explaining why operating margins were so high at 62.8%. The top five best-selling titles for the quarter were Devil May Cry 5 (released in 2019), Resident Evil 4 (released in 2005), Resident Evil Village (released in 2021), Street Fighter 6 (released in 2023), and Resident Evil 7: Biohazard (released in 2017), respectively. And it's worth noting that these were the same top five best-selling titles from the previous quarter, though in a slightly different order.

And while physical and mobile sales declined, given the company's revenue mix, the decline had virtually no impact on Capcom's profitability given their mix of revenue.

Other Segments of Revenue

Amusement equipments, which made up 15.4% of the company's total revenue for the nine-month period and delivered the strongest performance out of all segments last quarter, delivered another solid quarter of significant revenue and profit growth. Net sales are 74% YoY to ¥17.7 billion, with operating profit more than doubling—up 107% YoY to ¥10.5 billion and resulting in an operating margin of 59.4%—already by far exceeding the company's full-year plan. And what drove another stellar performance for this segment was the strong performance of new and repeat sales of pachislo machines that include the Devil May Cry 5 Stylish Tribe, which sold 11,000 units, and Shin Onimusha 3, which sold a whopping 24,500 units.

Arcade operations made up 16% of the company's total revenue for the nine-month period and posted steady results, similar to last quarter. Sales were up 12% YoY and operating profit was up 19% YoY. This was supported by same-store sales growth and a continued store expansion, including the opening of six new stores and the active expansion of new business formats.

Finally, other businesses, which made up 4.8% of the company's total revenue for the nine-month period and includes character licensing and media, grew its sales 58% YoY and has an operating profit that is up a whopping 135% YoY. This is a segment of revenue that was driven by both in-game collaborations and merchandise sales from the character business.

Cash, Spending, and Guidance

Despite an excellent overall earnings report, the company's cash flow is quite revealing for what lies up ahead. For starters, operating cash flow is down to –¥6.7 billion in comparison to last year's ¥18.9 billion inflow. Nevertheless, this was driven by an increase of ¥21.9 billion in work-in-progress for game software given the company's accelerated development ahead of its major upcoming releases. We can see this expanded development, along with the infrastructure expansion due to the aforementioned continued store expansion, reflected in the company's capital expenditures that are up a whopping 203.6% YoY to ¥10.9 billion—more than triple the prior year's numbers.

But, more importantly for investors, management affirmed its full-year guidance, a clear signal that the company's upcoming releases scheduled for late February and early March will translate all this increasing development spend into both revenue and profit for the final quarter of the fiscal year.

My Analysis of Capcom's Earnings

The Digital Contents Segment of Revenue

Capcom's Q3 results highlight both my favorite part about the company and something that I believe the market continues to underestimate: And that's the fact that this is a digitally scaled IP-business that has proven its ability to expand its earnings without recent major releases.

To me, the defining feature of these latest quarterly numbers is not the top-line growth but, rather, the sales mix. As mentioned above during the earnings recap, older games represented 96.4% of total game units sold from catalog titles, while 94.1% of all these units sold were digital downloads. This is a beautiful combination, from an investor's perspective, because the development and marketing costs for older game titles have already been incurred from years prior, meaning that these incremental digital sales lead to vastly higher profit margins. The result speaks for itself: a 62.8% operating margin for the digital contents segment of revenue in spite of a YoY decline in the sales of new titles and a decline in both physical and mobile sales.

This structure also explains why Capcom grew its operating profit 75% YoY on only 30% revenue growth. Whereas for many video game publishers where fewer new games may result in concerns about the company's demand, in Capcom's case, it has quite the opposite effect: margins expand given the company's high-quality games and the replayability that come from titles within their strongest franchises including Resident Evil, Monster Hunter, Devil May Cry, and Street Fighter. And these strong catalog sales, in turn, expand the company's margins and allows them to defer their launch-related marketing spend for future releases.

Other Segments of Revenue

But the company's performance outside of their digital contents segment of revenue also helps to smooth the company's earnings over time because they're essentially monetizing the same franchises on a separate timeline from their game releases. This does three vital things at once: It generates cash flow, it brings brand engagement, and it extends the economic life of Capcom's IP. For example, one of the best selling pachislo machines is the Devil May Cry 5 Stylish Tribe. The brand awareness from this pachislo machine not only brings consumer awareness to their Devil May Cry 5 video game, which currently sits at the top of their unit sales ranking for the fiscal year, but it also brings consumer awareness to their new season for the Devil May Cry anime series on Netflix (NFLX) that is set to release this upcoming May. This, ultimately, helps to reduce the company's dependency solely on their game releases, which is a structural advantage that pure game publishers do not have, and it also gives cultural relevance to the franchise even when no new games have been released.

Upcoming Catalysts

Even though Capcom's quarterly numbers are still fresh, I'm already looking forward to seeing their Q4 numbers for two reasons: Both Resident Evil Requiem and Monster Hunter Stories 3: Twisted Reflection are set for release.

Starting with Resident Evil Requiem, if you've followed me as an analyst, then you know that I have said, especially in my last article on Nintendo (NTDOY) (NTDOF), that Capcom's Resident Evil Requiem was the best-looking game in their Nintendo's latest Direct and was the game highlighted as the grand surprise during the event. It was by far the most impressive trailer in Nintendo's presentation promoting the Switch 2; it felt like watching a creepy suspense or horror-type movie trailer more than anything else. Well that game is set for release in less than one month. Let's not forget that three of Capcom's best-selling games for the fiscal year are from the Resident Evil franchise.

Meanwhile, Monster Hunter Stories 3: Twisted Reflection is set for release on March 13. This is a franchise that falls only second to Resident Evil in terms of total cumulative sales, having sold a total of 125 million units. Three of Capcom's top ten games in terms of sales for the fiscal year come from the Monster Hunter series, so investors should fully expect the series' latest installment to serve as another catalog accelerant and earnings driver.

So, if we take all of these upcoming catalysts into consideration, along with management reaffirming their full-year guidance, there is no doubt Q3 does not represent a peak for the company's earnings; rather, all this seems to be setting the stage for another record quarter going into Q4.

Valuation

Below is my EV to EBITDA peer comparison table with Capcom highlighted at the top. As we can see, the company trades at a multiple of 16.27, which is slightly above the median multiple of 15.74, an improvement from my last earnings article where the company was trading below the median, which indicates that the company is now fairly valued amongst its peers. And if you would like to see the company's reinvestment metrics to pair them alongside this updated table, you may find them in my previous earnings article on the company. But, overall, the reinvestment metrics for the company are excellent given that they are a low capital-intensive business, with excellent capital efficiency, and are able to convert a lot of their operating earnings into cash for further reinvestment.

An Updated Price Target

In my previous analysis, I gave Capcom a 12-month price target of $17.47, which at the time indicated 40.2% upside. In this updated model, we'll use the same EV to EBITDA target multiple of 20, and, using Seeking Alpha's EBITDA forward growth rate of 15.47% and applying it to the company's current TTM EBITDA, we get a forward EBITDA of $692 million. (Japanese yen are converted into U.S. dollars in this model). This gets us an implied enterprise value of $13.84 billion.

We then add back the current net cash for the company of $726 million, which gets us an implied equity value of $14.57 billion. And, given the ADR count for the company below, this gives us an updated 12-month price target of $17.41, representing 38% upside from the company's current share price of $12.63 as of the time of this writing.

Investor Takeaway

In sum, Capcom is not a hit-driven publisher, as in the case of CD Projekt (OTGLY) (OTGLF), but is, rather, a high-margin IP compounder that has a solid performing catalog of digital game sales to go along with a disciplined capital allocation. And Q3 confirms this durable—not cyclical—earnings strength with margins that continue to expand ahead of two major releases set for Q4. And given these Q4 catalysts, management reaffirming its guidance, solid earnings momentum from a record Q2, and with a realistic 38% upside given the company's current numbers, I reaffirm my continued strong buy rating for Capcom Co., Ltd.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: LG Display: Core Profit Beat And Memory Headwinds Draw Attention (Downgrade)
data.description:
I've revised LG Display Co., Ltd.'s (LPL) (034220.KS) rating from "Buy" to "Hold."

Tailwinds pertaining to its client and rivals were outlined in my previous Oct. 9, 2025, article. These have helped LPL record solid results in the final quarter of last year.

But the company's panels are used for products, for which memory is a key component. There's a big risk of its '26 results being dragged down by weaker-than-anticipated smartphone and laptop volumes.

Q4 Exceeded Expectations Excluding Exceptional Charges

LPL's latest quarterly financials were released on Wednesday, Jan 28.

Its reported EBIT for Oct-Dec '25 came in at ₩0.17 trillion. This implied a +103% year-on-year jump and a -61% sequential drop. Bloomberg's consensus projection of ₩430 billion was +153% higher.

The firm's analyst briefing commentary revealed that the "total nonrecurring cost impact in Q4 was in the high KRW 300 billion range." These were linked to a "voluntary retirement program" and "incentive payments tied to last year's business performance." The latter follows from LPL's return to a positive bottom line in FY25 after being in the red for the preceding three years.

I assume one-offs amounted to ₩380 billion, given that the exact figure wasn't shared. Its adjusted operating earnings of ₩550 billion would have beaten the sell-side's forecast by 28%. That's equivalent to QoQ and YoY increases of +27% and +562%, respectively.

My Oct 9 piece indicated that "LPL will perform well in FY25 on the back of a stronger-than-anticipated wearable product cycle." I also noted "the latest watches rolled out by Apple (AAPL) mentioned above come with Organic Light-Emitting Diode or OLED screens." These positives are reflected in the company's 4Q numbers.

Its "Average Selling Price/ASP" rose +14% YoY to $1.3k/sqm in the recent three-month period.

The proportion of 4Q25 group-level turnover generated from OLED-related solutions was almost two-thirds. This represented a +500bps (basis points) improvement as compared to 4Q2024.

In my opinion, the above-mentioned drivers of LPL's core EBIT beat are still intact for the near term.

Management has guided for a 1Q26 ASP of $1,200+. That's a 49% jump compared to the same period last year.

I also think LPL can continue to optimize its sales structure with a tilt towards high-value OLED products. At the results meeting, it emphasized the "strong and sustained upgrade to OLED-centric business structure." One move in this direction is the "strengthening" of its "OLED lineup for TV and monitors" by working more closely with "global strategic partners."

My forecasts point to the enterprise's operating income tripling to ₩100 billion in 1Q2026. I've assumed pricing improvement as per internal guidance and a similarly favorable OLED-to-LCD mix going forward.

But You Should Be Worried About Challenges With Key End-Markets

I'm much less optimistic about LPL's full-year showing, due to the memory boom.

It derived two-fifths and 36% of 4Q2025 topline from the "Mobile" and "Information Technology/IT" segments, respectively.

According to "Summit Research's" Jan. 27, 2026 write-up, "memory" can account for "up to 15% of the best-selling iPhone Pro variant’s BOM (Bill of Materials)."

You would expect appetite for LPL's products to wane. That can happen if handset makers are compelled to phase out low-margin models to preserve profitability. BofA (BAC) recently issued a report (not publicly available) titled "Memory Inflation" on Jan. 21. As per the bank's channel checks, leading Chinese OEMs "Xiaomi (XIACY) and Transsion" have "collectively cut (this year's) shipment plans by 40-100M units."

LPL might also see its margins contract due to smartphone specification downgrades involving the usage of lower-grade panels.

Separately, IT products like "Personal Computers/PCs" should also be affected.

A Jan 23 Jefferies (JEF) note (inaccessible in public) with the name "Memory Prices" highlighted a "10-20% PC price increases" in '26 year-to-date. It's inevitable that rising component costs have to be passed on to end-users. But that is likely to translate into lower computer-related panel sales.

LPL acknowledged at the Q4 call that the "memory price" uptrend may possibly "dampen demand" and compel "customers to lower the panel price."

The firm's FY25 EBIT adjusted for one-offs is about ₩0.9 trillion. The analysts' 2026 consensus estimate is ₩1,190 billion (source: S&P Capital IQ), implying a +32% growth.

My own FY26 prediction is a substantially lower ₩0.8 trillion, considering memory-related headwinds.

My Concluding Remarks

I'm becoming "Neutral" on this name.

LPL is reasonably valued based on the "Gordon Growth Model Formula." My inputs are a 9.9% ROE (consensus forecast), a 3% growth rate (mature economy), and an 11% cost of equity (high to reflect 141% net gearing).

That gives me a target P/B of 0.86 times. The stock is now trading at a similar 0.87x as per S&P Capital IQ.

The core 4Q25 beat is impressive, and it should still grow at a fast pace in 1Q26.

But my view is that LPL's full-year results could be disappointing. I perceive it as one of the memory cycle's victims.

data.title: ECB Preview: The First Real 'Global Euro Moment'
data.description:
By Carsten Brzeski, Global Head of Macro

Last May, ECB President Christine Lagarde delivered a motivational speech aiming to unleash pro-European sentiment and support for deeper integration, calling for a ‘global euro moment’. With the recent weakening of the US dollar - and the corresponding strengthening of the euro - next week’s ECB meeting could be more interesting than imagined just a few days ago.

ECB still sits comfortably in its 'good place'

When it comes to the basics of monetary policy, given everything currently going on in the world, the ECB has almost become a beacon of continuity - some might even say boredom. The ECB simply calls it a ‘good place’, i.e., a eurozone economy that looks set to grow at around potential and an inflation rate settling around target. Data releases since the December meeting have only confirmed that view.

What's not to like? Well, maybe the high level of uncertainty in both geopolitics and economic outcomes. Up until now, there has been a clear disconnect between geopolitics and macroeconomics. No one knows whether this disconnect will hold or whether one side of the equation will eventually move. Geopolitical risks could slow down, or the economy could eventually still weaken. However, as long as these geopolitical risks and uncertainties do not translate into substantial changes to the eurozone outlook, the ECB will watch but not act.

Stronger euro could revive debate on possible rate cut

In recent days, however, market developments have started to make the ECB’s 'good place' a little less comfortable. The weakening of the US dollar and hence the strengthening of the euro have led to some unease at the Bank. Austrian central bank governor Martin Kocher said that "if the euro appreciates further and further, at some stage this might create, of course, a certain necessity to react in terms of monetary policy. But not because of the exchange rate itself, but because the exchange rate translates into less inflation, and then this is, of course, a monetary policy issue." French central bank governor, Francois Villeroy de Galhau, hinted that the stronger euro was a key factor in guiding monetary policy in the months ahead. And ECB vice-president Luis de Guindos said last summer that an exchange rate of 1.20 versus the dollar was acceptable but levels beyond that could become more complicated. Well, we are at that level now.

From a monetary policy perspective, the recent strengthening of the euro would lower the ECB’s December inflation forecasts by some 0.1 percentage point. Compared with December, the euro has appreciated by some 3.5 percent vis-à-vis the US dollar and by some 1.5 percent vis-à-vis a basket of currencies (nominal effective exchange rate). All else equal, the ECB’s projections would now show headline inflation falling below 2% for the next three years – a development that is likely to revive concerns among those Governing Council members who have long feared an inflation undershoot and view it as a risk to the ECB’s credibility.

Is Europe ready for a 'global euro moment'?

More broadly speaking, these latest reactions show that not everyone at the ECB (or in Europe) is ready for a global euro moment. In fact, the recent strengthening of the euro - and we are talking about strengthening, not yet outright strength - illustrates that Europe should be careful what it wishes for. It is hard to reconcile the ambition of a global euro with an export-orientated economy. A global currency normally comes with relative currency strength, deep and liquid financial markets, and often military power. In fact, a global euro should not be a goal in itself but rather the result of a capital markets union, fiscal union and strategic autonomy. If all these foundations are achieved, the euro will be even more attractive and the eurozone economy will be far better placed to absorb a stronger currency.

If the euro strengthens further, the chances of rate cut in March would increase

For now and for next week, the stronger euro is a complicating factor for the cyclical turnaround in industry, which is just materialising, and for the growth outlook in general. The recent appreciation will not be a big enough concern for the ECB to change course next week. For now, the central bank will stay in its good place, and we don’t expect Lagarde to say anything more on the exchange rate, beyond noting that the ECB will monitor it closely.

However, if the latest trend continues and if the ECB wants to send a signal that a slight undershoot of inflation is as much a concern as a slight overshoot, the chances of a rate cut in March would clearly increase.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: AMD: Expensive For A Reason, Upgrade Before Earnings
data.description:
It appears that timing of my previous neutral AMD (AMD) thesis was quite good as the share price remained at approximately the same level since November while the S&P 500 Index delivered a 1.6% increase. AMD is set to release its Q4 earnings next week, on February 3rd after the market close. While AMD's valuation remains stretched since there were no notable positive earnings revisions recently, I think that there might be new ones after the upcoming earnings release as I expect Lisa Su to speak about soaring demand for AI accelerators during the earnings call. Recent earnings of the two large data center investors, Microsoft and Meta, clearly showed that hyperscalers do not plan to cut AI capital investments in the foreseeable future. Moreover, we see that China is set to resume buying chips from the U.S. giants, which is another potential large growth driver for AMD. Therefore, I believe that AMD's elevated multiples are justified and the stock deserves an upgrade to 'Buy' despite being the laggard compared to Nvidia (NVDA). The addressable market is expanding that fast so it will be enough space for both NVDA and AMD to capitalize on the secular trend.

Q4 earnings preview

First and foremost, AMD is very strong in beating consensus estimates almost every quarter. The company's earnings history suggests that there were only three EPS misses and only one revenue miss over the last four years, which means that the win rate is high. The win rate alone doesn't mean that a new double earnings beat is guaranteed on February 3rd, but it underscores the management commitment to deliver on its financial targets. Moreover, there are robust fundamental factors supporting the expectation of a solid double earnings beat next week.

Wall Street analysts are optimistic, which I see from the recent earnings revisions setup outlined above. Analysts forecast AMD's Q4 revenue to amount to $9.67 billion, which will be a 26% year-over-year increase. The EPS is expected to deliver a 20%+ growth as well by increasing from $1.09 to $1.32. These projected increases look quite conservative given AMD's strong growth momentum in three previous quarters when the company maintained a 30%+ revenue growth.

Based on recent developments in the industry, I tend to agree with Wall Street's optimism. Microsoft and Meta released their Q4 earnings yesterday, and they were marked with each company's management's ambitious plans regarding expanding their AI compute capacity. Microsoft demonstrated impressive revenue growth in cloud business and substantial Copilot adoption during the quarter. The management reiterated a strategic emphasis on AI by broadening its ecosystem of offerings, which is likely to support elevated CapEx investments. And Microsoft's management is confident that aggressive investments in AI infrastructure will payoff because CFO Amy Hood stated, 'the majority of the capital that we're spending today... are already contracted for most of their useful life'.

Meta's management also sees value in their aggressive investments in AI. During the earnings call, Mark Zuckerberg cited 'AI-driven performance gains' and there was a massive FY2026 capital expenditures guidance between $115 billion and $135 billion. The company's CapEx outlook is notably higher compared to guidance provided in Q3, which means that Meta shifts to even more aggressive AI infrastructure investment. Since both MSFT and META power their data centers with AMD's EPYC processors, yesterday's earnings calls are certainly bullish for the company. Therefore, I expect AMD to not only deliver another double beat next week, but I also expect the management to release an upbeat FY2026 guidance.

Moreover, let's not forget that there is another large catalyst on the horizon, which is the strong demand for high-end American chips from Chinese tech giants. During the previous earnings call, AMD's management spoke about Chinese market with caution because at that time the level of geopolitical uncertainty was still quite high. However, today this uncertainty does not look that bad because in December Lisa Su confirmed that AMD has licenses to ship its MI308 chips to China. Of course, there is competition in China from the local chip manufacturers, but the fact that Chinese government recently gave nod to the country's tech giants to order Nvidia's H200 processors suggests that the demand is too strong so local suppliers are unable to meet it either from the volume perspective or from computing power perspective. Therefore, I think that AMD can capitalize on soaring demand in China as well.

Mitigating factors

It is quite difficult to predict whether there will be a strong positive reaction from investors post-earnings as AMD's current valuation is stretched, meaning that big optimism is already priced in. The company's estimated FY2028 forward P/E ratio looks quite good at 18, but to achieve such dramatic P/E contraction AMD needs to deliver aggressive EPS growth over several years ahead.

While there are extremely strong AI tailwinds that will support AMD's growth, it is crucial to keep in mind that AMD is not the biggest fish in the pond. Nvidia is a much stronger player based on its scale and financial power, which means that AMD will highly likely be able to capitalize only on what is left after Nvidia takes its large piece of the pie. The demand is so strong at the moment and Nvidia doesn't have enough capacity to capture even a larger share of the market. However, with their $60 billion cash pile (~9 times larger than AMD's) and $53 billion TTM free cash flow (~18 times larger than AMD's), it is in a much stronger financial position to invest in growth. Therefore, over the long-term there is a significant risk of Nvidia significantly boosting its capacity to conquer a larger market share at AMD's expense.

Moreover, there is extremely high level of uncertainty regarding the longevity of the current data center spending spree. Of course, tech behemoths will continue investing in AI infrastructure until AI adoption is strong and they see robust revenue growth and profitability expansion due to cross-selling AI capabilities. However, it is impossible to forecast for how long their AI-related revenues will soar and justify aggressive data center spending.

Conclusion

I think that we are likely to see a solid earnings release from AMD next week with a double beat on headline numbers coupled with an upbeat guidance based on soaring demand for AMD's data center business. After a strong earnings release there might be a big share price movement, but I am not ready to give AMD a 'Strong buy' rating because over the long haul there is a huge competition risk posed by Nvidia. Therefore, my take is that AMD is a 'Buy' at the moment.
